name: Cabañeros National Park and Torre de Abraham Reservoir Loop
name_original: Embalse Torre de Abraham desde Horcajo de los Montes – circular por Parque Nacional de Cabañeros
description: This difficult 75.5-mile road cycling route circles Embalse Torre de Abraham through Parque Nacional de Cabañeros, offering rich wildlife.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Alcoba are situated within a region characterized by rolling hills and extensive natural parks, notably the Cabañeros National Park. The terrain features a mix of open countryside, dense woodlands, and river valleys, providing varied landscapes for cyclists. Elevation changes are common, with routes often including significant ascents and descents. The area's roads are generally quiet, offering a focused cycling experience through natural environments. Best road cycling routes around Alcoba The most popular road cycling route is Montes de Toledo Circuit, a 75.8 miles (122.0 km) trail that takes 5 hours 10 minutes to complete. This difficult route features nearly 3,181 feet (969 meters) of elevation gain through the Montes de Toledo. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Fontanarejo from Horcajo de los Montes — circular by the río Valdehornos and Alcoba de Montes, a moderate 35.7 miles (57.4 km) path. This route offers scenic views along the río Valdehornos and through the Alcoba de Montes area. Local road cyclists also love the Cabañeros National Park and Torre de Abraham Reservoir Loop, a 75.5 miles (121.5 km) trail leading through Cabañeros National Park, often completed in about 5 hours 4 minutes. Road cycling around Alcoba is defined by rolling hills, dense woodlands, and reservoir loops. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging routes with significant elevation. The routes in Alcoba are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 2.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 40 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Alcoba's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Alcoba?
A: Komoot currently features 4 road cycling routes around Alcoba. These routes offer a variety of experiences, from moderate rides to more challenging excursions through the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Alcoba?
A: Road cycling routes in Alcoba are characterized by rolling hills, dense woodlands, and river valleys. You'll encounter a mix of open countryside and natural parks, such as the Cabañeros National Park. Elevation changes are common, with routes often including significant ascents and descents, providing a varied and engaging cycling experience.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders in Alcoba?
A: Yes, Alcoba offers several challenging road cycling routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1371434336/montes-de-toledo-circuit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montes de Toledo Circuit&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 75.8-mile (122.0 km) trail with nearly 3,181 feet (969 meters) of elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1067730682/cabaneros-national-park-and-torre-de-abraham-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabañeros National Park and Torre de Abraham Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 75.5 miles (121.5 km) through the national park.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes in Alcoba?
A: Yes, there are several circular road cycling routes available. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1179507019/fontanarejo-from-horcajo-de-los-montes-circular-by-the-rio-valdehornos-and-alcoba-de-montes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fontanarejo from Horcajo de los Montes — circular by the río Valdehornos and Alcoba de Montes&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 35.7-mile (57.4 km) path offering scenic views. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1366179733/from-horcajo-de-los-montes-through-the-cabaneros-national-park-circular-route-around-the-torre-de-abraham-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Horcajo de los Montes through the Cabañeros National Park — circular route around the Torre de Abraham Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see while road cycling in Alcoba?
A: While road cycling in Alcoba, you can explore various natural attractions. The routes often pass through the Cabañeros National Park and along the río Valdehornos. Notable highlights in the area include the beautiful &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5634889" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Chorrera de Horcajo Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5857156" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Rubial Stream&lt;/a&gt;, and the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6325378" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Grandfather Cork Oak&lt;/a&gt;. You might also encounter charming settlements like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5647847" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Robledo&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5577922" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alcoba de los Montes&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in Alcoba?
A: The road cycling routes in Alcoba are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 2.0 stars from over 1 review. More than 40 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Alcoba's varied terrain, often praising the quiet roads and the immersive experience through natural environments like the Montes de Toledo and Cabañeros National Park.

Q: Is it possible to find long-distance road cycling routes in Alcoba?
A: Yes, Alcoba offers several long-distance road cycling routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1371434336/montes-de-toledo-circuit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montes de Toledo Circuit&lt;/a&gt; is a substantial 75.8-mile (122.0 km) ride, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1067730682/cabaneros-national-park-and-torre-de-abraham-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabañeros National Park and Torre de Abraham Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; is nearly as long at 75.5 miles (121.5 km), providing excellent options for extended rides.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Alcoba?
A: Given the region's characteristics of rolling hills and natural parks, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in Alcoba. Temperatures are milder, and the natural landscapes are vibrant. Summers can be hot, while winters might bring cooler temperatures, so checking local weather forecasts is always recommended.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in Alcoba?
A: While many routes in Alcoba feature significant elevation changes, there are moderate options that could be suitable for families with some cycling experience. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1179507019/fontanarejo-from-horcajo-de-los-montes-circular-by-the-rio-valdehornos-and-alcoba-de-montes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fontanarejo from Horcajo de los Montes — circular by the río Valdehornos and Alcoba de Montes&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate route that offers scenic views without the extreme difficulty of some longer trails.

Q: Are there any permits required for road cycling in the Alcoba region?
A: Generally, no specific permits are required for recreational road cycling on public roads in the Alcoba region. However, if your route takes you through specific protected areas or private land, it's always advisable to check local regulations or signage for any restrictions or permit requirements. For routes within Cabañeros National Park, standard park rules apply.

Q: Can I find places to eat or stay near the road cycling routes in Alcoba?
A: The Alcoba region, with its charming settlements like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5647847" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Robledo&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5577922" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alcoba de los Montes&lt;/a&gt;, offers opportunities to find local cafes, restaurants, and accommodation. These villages often provide a welcoming stop for cyclists looking to refuel or stay overnight. It's recommended to check availability in advance, especially in smaller towns.

Q: Where can I find viewpoints along the road cycling routes in Alcoba?
A: Many routes in Alcoba traverse areas with natural beauty, offering scenic vistas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1179507019/fontanarejo-from-horcajo-de-los-montes-circular-by-the-rio-valdehornos-and-alcoba-de-montes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fontanarejo from Horcajo de los Montes&lt;/a&gt; route, for example, provides views along the río Valdehornos. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4319882" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cañada Real Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; is a specific highlight in the area known for its panoramic views.
tips: Horcajo has a unique charm: its municipal term is integrated into the Cabañeros National Park. 1Km from the urban center of Horcajo, is the largest visitor center in the Park, essential to plan a visit. There, a main exhibition shows the different ecosystems of Cabañeros throughout the four seasons of the year. In addition, a farmhouse in which the culture and traditions of the region are disclosed. It is completed with projection rooms, a library, a picnic area, car parks, etc.

It is considered a natural and tourist gem in Castilla-La Mancha. It stands out for its spectacular landscape, tranquility, and biodiversity. Ideal for photography, hiking, fishing, and water sports. Visitors appreciate the accessible riverside path, the panoramic viewpoint, and its proximity to the Torre de Abraham Visitor Center, which offers information about the flora, fauna, and history of the park.
Retuerta del Bullaque is a small town next to the Bullaque river. The town is considered one of the gateways to the Cabañeros National Park on the slopes of Mount Toledo.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a difficult road cycling route, spanning 122 kilometers with an elevation gain of 850 meters. It is considered a 'hard road ride' and requires a very good level of physical conditioning.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The estimated completion time for this 122-kilometer circular route is approximately 5 hours and 4 minutes for an experienced road cyclist.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: No, given its length (122 km), significant elevation gain (850 m), and 'difficult' rating, this route is not suitable for beginners or families. It is designed for experienced road cyclists with a high level of fitness.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this route in Horcajo de los Montes?
faqs.answer: The route starts in Horcajo de los Montes. While specific parking areas aren't detailed, as a village that hosts a visitor center for the national park, you should find suitable parking options within or on the outskirts of the village. It's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The route predominantly features well-paved surfaces, making it generally easy to ride for road cyclists. You'll be cycling through diverse landscapes, including plains and mountains.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife can I expect to see in Parque Nacional de Cabañeros?
faqs.answer: Parque Nacional de Cabañeros is known as the 'Serengeti of Spain' due to its rich biodiversity. You might spot large birds of prey, black storks, eagles, and vultures. The park also protects Mediterranean forest and dehesa ecosystems, offering a chance to see various protected species.

faqs.question: Are there any notable viewpoints or landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers several points of interest. You'll pass through Horcajo de los Montes, home to the Parish Church of San Antonio Abad. A major highlight is the Torre de Abraham Reservoir, known for its tranquil environment and picturesque views. You can also enjoy a View of the Montes de Toledo from Retuerta del Bullaque.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder and the natural scenery is at its most vibrant. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be cold and wet, making cycling less comfortable.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to cycle through Parque Nacional de Cabañeros?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific entrance fees or permits required for cycling on public roads within Parque Nacional de Cabañeros. However, it's always a good idea to check the official park website or visitor center in Horcajo de los Montes for any updated regulations or specific access restrictions, especially for off-road trails if you plan to deviate from the main route.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with sections of other trails. You'll encounter parts of the Camino de Cabañeros - Etapa 1, Ruta del Río Bullaque, Camino de Cabañeros - Etapa 2, Cañada Real Galiana; Cañada Real Riojana, Cañada Real Segoviana, and Cañada Real Toledana.

faqs.question: Is the Embalse Torre de Abraham from Horcajo de los Montes route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed in many parts of Parque Nacional de Cabañeros, specific rules apply, especially within protected areas and on designated trails. For this road cycling route, dogs would need to be able to keep pace with a bicycle for 122 km, which is highly unlikely. If you plan to bring a dog, it's best to confirm park regulations regarding pets on roads and ensure their safety and well-being for such a long distance.

faqs.question: Are there any places to stop for refreshments or food along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through villages like Horcajo de los Montes and El Robledo. These villages are likely to have small shops, cafes, or restaurants where you can refuel. However, given the length of the route, it's always wise to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially between villages, as services might be sparse.
